TextENGNew: Sunday.
The sound of fireworks I heard before noon was probably an announcement that the festival had opened.
That day... the weather could not, by any stretch, be called festival weather. It was overcast.
The television forecast warned that things might turn into a downpour from evening through midnight. But as long as it wasn't raining now, the festival would go ahead.
The festival had to be held... or nothing could begin.
The grounds of the Furude Shrine were probably all decked out by now—dressed up for the one grand occasion of the year.
The auspicious red-and-white curtains. The paper lanterns hanging in clusters.
changeReason: Old merges 紅白幕 (red-and-white curtains) and 提灯 (lanterns) into one item; new correctly separates them as two distinct decorations.

TextENGNew: ...The hum of generators powering the food stalls.
...The voices of children running about.
Families watching them, smiling. Laughter.@k ...Today was the Watanagashi festival.
......Was everyone... already out there enjoying the festivities?
What about Satoko?
...Had she set aside the suffering of recent days, if only for a little while... and was she showing that radiant smile I hadn't seen in so long?
...Today would probably be the longest day of my life.
A day I would remember vividly for as long as I lived.
My parents were planning to head to the festival in the evening, spending a leisurely early afternoon at home.
...Living under the same roof, I couldn't help but marvel at how differently we were spending this time.
...Paying them no mind... I headed for the front door.
I tied my laces a little tighter than usual.@k ...As if the firmness of the knot expressed the firmness of my resolve.
......When I first resolved to kill Satoko's uncle... I was in such a state of excitement I thought I might lose my mind.
...And when I began planning the act of murder—an act that went directly against every moral I had ever been taught... I was in such a state of calm it felt as though I had lost all emotion.
...And then... yesterday.
......When I learned that Satoshi had made the exact same phone call as me...@k ...I was left with a tangle of feelings I couldn't even begin to describe.
...And now, today had arrived.
Right now, every emotion I had carried until yesterday had drained away.
...If I had to put it simply... it was like that feeling right after waking up, still groggy, when none of your emotions have sharpened into focus.
I felt no anger toward that man.
No sorrow or pity for Satoko.
No resentment toward my friends, who had only watched and waited, never once reaching out to help... and no tension or fear about the day that had finally come.
...Yes.@k My current state... could be called the ideal condition for carrying out a murder.
When the moment came, I would probably need to surrender myself to violent emotion.
...But up until that very instant... I would be like an insect.@k ...Silently, methodically inching toward my sole objective...... and in the moment I caught my prey, I would strike like a bullet.
There was no emotion in any of that.
...Just the eerie, insectile coldness.@k ...That was the best state of mind to have.
...I would kill Satoko's uncle like a worm.@k It wasn't lost on me—the irony of thinking that, while I myself had the mindset of an insect.
I retied my laces one more time... and stepped outside.
I took the shovel from the storage shed out front.@k I would need it to dig that man's grave.
It was a convenient camping shovel... If I twisted it and broke it down into three pieces, I could easily hide it in my bag.
I twisted the shovel... and took it apart.
I'd done it plenty of times before, yet... for some reason, I struggled.@k ...As if I'd suddenly become clumsy.
......I knew why.
...It wasn't nerves.
...The cowardly part of me, buried deep inside, was balking.
...It knew that the act of dismantling this shovel... was the first step toward murder.
The final twist was especially stubborn... but it yielded at last before the full force of my determination...
The area around Satoko's house... was outside my usual range of activity.
...Just riding my bike through here in broad daylight... might raise suspicion from anyone paying attention.
I absolutely wanted to avoid running into anyone I knew and having them call out to me...
With that in mind, I carefully chose my route to the destination.
...A bit of a detour didn't bother me.@k ...On a day as long as this one, I could never be too careful...
...Still, I was fortunate not to cross paths with a single person on my way to the planned crime scene.
...I wasn't about to start believing in omens now... but it was a good sign.
The place I had chosen to dig the hole... was in this grove, deeper into the woods.
...I worried I might not find the same spot again in a forest with no landmarks... but I arrived there without any trouble.
I looked around once more.
...Not the slightest trace of another person.@k ...The air was a little damp, but the shade of the trees provided a pleasant coolness.
I took the disassembled shovel from my bag... and put it back together with practiced hands.
...Then... I placed the tip of the shovel against a soft-looking patch of ground... and set my foot on it.
...If I put my weight into it, the shovel would bite deep into the earth.
...But that act of plunging it in... felt like an irreversible threshold... and it made me hesitate.
I was only digging a hole with a shovel... yet I had to force down a hard swallow.
...Calm down, Keiichi Maebara.
...Okay, match your breathing.@k ...On the count of three, drive it in.@k ......Ready... one... two...... ngh...
I couldn't get the timing right, and for something as simple as driving a shovel into the ground... I had to try five, six times.
...Damn it... it's just digging a hole... this is pathetic!
As I thought that... I stomped my heel down as if crushing something loathsome, and a sharp "crack!!" echoed out.
...The ground was soft only at the start.
...Roots and stones quickly got in the way, and I was forced to confront just how hard it was to dig a hole large enough to bury a person.
No decent person would ever come to a place like this...
Wouldn't it be enough to just pile some dirt over him and call it done...?
...Every time such a weak thought surfaced, I would clench my teeth and drive the shovel in even harder.
This is enough...@k I could hide him with this much...
Each time I thought that, I would climb down into the hole, only to be dismayed by how shallow it still was, and resume digging.
...The thin sheen of sweat on my brow had finally gathered into drops that rolled down my face.
...My back was even worse.
My shirt clung to my skin... unbearably disgusting.
...But what I couldn't stand the most were the mosquitoes.
...Drawn by the scent of my sweat from who knows where... the grimy black-and-white striped mosquitoes clung to me every time I let my guard down.
It was hot.
It was revolting.
I was sticky.
...And itchy.@k ...Waves of discomfort crashed over me, one after another.
"...Damn it all...@k@vS03/01/140100636. So what... you want me to quit and go home?!@k@vS03/01/140100637. Is that it?!"
I wasn't yelling at anyone.@k Just talking to myself. Telling myself.
...Why was I... enduring all this... digging a hole in a place like this...?
What did I stand to gain from digging this hole?
What did I stand to lose from not digging it?
And in the first place... who ordered me to dig?
...Thinking back... maybe ever since elementary school, or even before that... my entire life had been built on doing what others told me to do.
...That didn't mean living by others' orders had felt restrictive.
Nor did it mean I'd been too lazy to search for my own path.
It was more like... yes... a road.
I'd never been particularly gifted physically.
...I wasn't an early bloomer, and when we lined up by height in class, I was rarely taller than the middle.
My records in jump-rope competitions and marathons were the very picture of average.
Nobody ever fought over having me on their dodgeball team.
Those kindergarten and early elementary years, when physical ability and rate of growth seemed to determine a person's entire worth... even now, remembering them felt deeply unpleasant.
What changed everything... was a single offhand remark from my mother.
"Keiichi.@k@vS03/00/142700050. Would you like to try going to cram school?@k@vS03/00/142700051. Once you understand what they teach at school, it'll become fun."
The very first test I took at cram school felt like a game—it was genuinely fun.
Unlike the school tests where the same kind of problem appeared dozens of times, every question came with illustrations—it was like a puzzle book that came as a bonus with a manga magazine.
If schoolwork had been like this, it would have been so much more enjoyable.
A few days later, I went back to the cram school with my mother to complete the enrollment.
...My mother and the cram school staff had a long, earnest discussion, and I grew drowsy and missed most of it... but this one part I still remember clearly.
My mother let out a startled exclamation.
"Um... when you say 61, do you mean his average score was 61 points?"

"No, not at all.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04002. His deviation value across all our cram schools nationwide is 61.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04003. Even on a simple IQ assessment, he shows extraordinary results.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04004. ...To put it plainly, Keiichi Maebara-kun is exceptionally intelligent."

"Keiichi...?@k@vS03/00/142700054. But his grades at school aren't anything special.@k@vS03/00/142700055. His report card is just a mix of 2s, 3s, and 4s—completely average...@k@vS03/00/142700056. ...Could there be some mistake...?"

"We cross-referenced with several intelligence tests, and the results revealed something very interesting.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04006. Maebara-kun is extremely weak when it comes to problems that lack meaningful context.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04007. ...He simply cannot engage with dry, abstract content that has no connection to real life."
"...So even if he can't solve 2+3, if you tell him he has two apples and gets three more... he can answer a question phrased that way?"

"Precisely.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04009. For instance, Keiichi-kun gets confused when told to draw the net of a regular hexahedron... but if you ask him what shape you'd get by cutting open a die—@k@vS03/00/jukuA04010. he can answer it perfectly."
Then the staff member showed her one of the test papers I'd completed.
...It was an answer sheet for a construction problem.@k Construction problems were so unusual that I'd found them really interesting.

"This question asks the student to draw the net of a regular icosahedron.@k@vS03/00/jukuA04012. ...When we rephrased it as: how would you cut cardboard to make a twenty-sided die?@k@vS03/00/jukuA04013. Keiichi-kun drew the answer as though it were child's play."
"...That is not something an ordinary child can do."
"Keiichi.@k@vS03/00/142700059. Have you ever made a twenty-sided die from a magazine supplement or anything like that?"
Dice only go from one to six.
Until that test, it had never even occurred to me that dice with more than six sides could exist.
So imagining what a die that went up to twenty would look like was fun.
If I had a die like that, I'd never lose at board games against anyone.@k So I immediately wanted to make one.
Before long, I enrolled.
...The class I was placed in had only four students, and was called the 'Select' class.

It was the highest-level class... and some of my school classmates were in the lower classes.@k When I remembered how much better they had done at dodgeball and the fifty-meter dash, I felt genuine happiness well up inside me for the first time.
Even if I was physically inferior, I had found another way to come out on top.
It was fun at first.
The more I studied, the more I was praised, and the sudden attention from my school teachers felt good.
...My parents seemed satisfied, too.@k Seeing them satisfied made me happy.
The more I followed my parents' directives, the more fun everything became.
That really did happen, didn't it.@k ...I laughed dryly, as if dismissing my own past.
Well... in the end, that studious life didn't last very long.
The enjoyment of being able to study more and more only lasted at the beginning.

I gradually stopped getting along with my friends...@k and I lost all respect for teachers whose lessons were always three steps behind what I was learning at cram school, presented in a way that somehow made everything dull.
...A smug brat who flaunted his academic prowess couldn't stay on top of the world forever.@k ...The world just wasn't that simple.
I followed orders, exceeded expectations, and was praised for it.
...The happiness of that cycle kept me spinning it like the wheels of a bicycle.
...I thought that pedaling forward like that was what life was.
It wasn't until I moved to Hinamizawa... that I realized how impoverished that life had been.
Various things happened, and the topic of moving somewhere for a change of scenery came up.
That was when my dad... mentioned a place he'd visited several times for plein air painting and grown fond of...
He started talking about wanting to set up his atelier there.
...And before I knew it, the move to Hinamizawa was decided.
And that was how...... I... met them.
My first day as a transfer student.
...I walked toward the classroom with the teacher.
...It felt pathetic to be led in by the teacher, so I reached the door before she did, threw it open with a bang, and strode into the classroom.
Clatter.
The bold way I opened that door... was nothing less than the strength of my resolve to start my life over.
...And then... within seconds, that resolve took a hard counterpunch to the face.
A blackboard eraser from above.
With a heavy rock stuffed inside, no less... a trap designed for maximum pain.
...Come on, seriously? I was already getting nailed by Satoko on my very first day?
That caught me off guard.@k I was completely blindsided.
The class itself was a surprise, too.
Not just because the students were a mix of different ages and genders.
...It was because the atmosphere was completely different from any school I had ever known.
And that sense of surprise... only grew as I spent my days with them.
That fresh wonder... had never faded, even up to today.
...Every day was an unbroken chain of fresh surprises... I hadn't had a single boring day since coming here.
The lively days spent with the club members.
We played Old Maid with marked cards.@k We went all out in Millionaire, holding nothing back.
We caused a ruckus competing against the public at tournaments in the town's toy shop, and so much more besides...
And as an extension of those days, there had been the lunchbox competition.
...That was when it happened.
When I noticed an unexpected side of Satoko.
Thinking back... I only ate dinner with Satoko two nights.
Even now, remembering them warmed my heart—such gentle, tender time.
I realized that Satoko, for all her brave front... was quietly waiting somewhere in her heart for her Nii-nii to come home.@k ...And I swore I would become her Nii-nii.
Satoko could be bossy and fussy... but she was a caring little sister at heart.
It may have looked like she was the one taking the lead, but that was actually Satoko's own way of leaning on someone.
The daily life Satoshi and Satoko shared as siblings... may have been constrained under their uncle and aunt... but it must have been warm all the same.
Those warm days... when did they go wrong? What mistake unraveled them?
I had told myself so many times that lamenting past tragedies wouldn't change anything... yet I lamented anyway.
So, Keiichi...@k To take back those days... you chose your own path, didn't you?
Nobody ordered you.@k You decided on your own.@k You weren't hoping for anyone's praise.
You thought it all through yourself.@k You're carving it out yourself.@k ...Even now!

This wasn't the kind of road parents or society had paved... a road where you could see far ahead.@k ...It was a narrow path in utter darkness, every next step shrouded in the most unsettling blackness.
But... that lonely path... led everywhere.
There was nowhere it couldn't reach... an infinite road.@k A road only those who could envision their own destination could choose.
Don't be ashamed of straying from the brightly paved highway.
Rather, take pride... in having found a path that is yours alone.
Besides, I hadn't abandoned the paved road.
...Once I achieved my goal, I would return to that bright road. And then... I would spend those ordinary, warm days at ease once more...
Crunch...
...When I snapped back to awareness, the hole had grown... unbelievably large and deep.
...Had I... really dug all this by myself...?
"...I think this is... enough, isn't it..."
I set the shovel down at the bottom of the hole.
...I would drag the body here, throw it in, and cover it with dirt.
...Even if it took time to camouflage the surface with brush and grass, it would be far easier than the digging had been.
I checked my watch.@k ...It was nearly evening before I realized it.
...I may not have felt the time passing...@k but enough hours had clearly elapsed to match the depth of this hole.
I sat down on a stone on a nearby slope... and wiped the sweat from my brow.
......Now... once it got a little darker, I would retrieve the metal bat hidden at the schoolyard.
...Then I would make the phone call to lure that man out.
......And then...... carry it out.
The steps remaining before that moment... were dwindling.
I felt that closing distance far more acutely than the mere passage of time.
...I had no hesitation, and yet I felt it—a contradiction.
...At every step of the process leading up to the act, I had chances to hesitate, to waver... to let myself be blown off course by cowardice.
Having to endure those million temptations while waiting for the moment... was a torture so slow and agonizing it defied description.
At first I was ashamed of my weakness, but eventually I stopped seeing it that way.
...This was fine.@k ...Just fine.
There was nothing wrong with feeling revulsion toward the aberrant act of taking a life.
...After all, I wasn't going to become a killer from today onward.
...I would pass through this one aberrant day and return to the world I belonged in.
If I sank to the level of a monster who felt nothing at the thought of killing, I could never go back to my old life.
...So this was fine.@k I didn't need to feel ashamed for hesitating to kill.
Because... I was human.
Here I was, carrying out Oyashiro-sama's curse on his behalf, and yet acutely aware of my own humanity... What a ridiculous irony...
I let out a light sigh... and stood up.
I erased my presence... and sharpened my senses in every direction... searching for any sign of another person...
...All clear.
...Let's go.@k ...One step at a time.@k I would walk my own path.@k ...A path no one had forced upon me.
"...Here I go...@k@vS03/01/140100641. ...Keiichi Maebara..."
The cicadas droned and buzzed, filling the air with their ceaseless noise.
...Their chorus drowned out the suspicious sound of me snapping twigs underfoot.
...And it filled the road far, far ahead, telling me there was no one around for a long distance.
The cicadas blessed the path I walked, cheering me on with every ounce of strength they had.
The sky was high.
...Before long, the curtain of night would descend and paint the sky in evening dusk.
...By then, the higurashi would surely take up their chorus in place of the cicadas.
By then... would everything already be settled?@k Would it all be over...?
I had said something similar when I entrusted Satoko's fate to the public agencies.
...But this time... was different.@k ...I would do it.@k I would carry it out.@k I would... end it.
It@|@y would@|@y all@|@y be@|@y over@|@y very@|@y soon.@r@|@yEverything would end.
